findings.description: Inverted expiry logic: returns age < ttl, so _is_expired reports True for fresh entries (which then get deleted) and False for stale ones (which are served forever).
Due to the inverted check, fresh L1 entries are deleted on every read while genuinely expired entries are returned, defeating both correctness and the caching benefit.
pickle.loads on data read from Redis enables arbitrary code execution if the L2 store is attacker-controllable or poisoned (CWE-502).
None is used as the miss sentinel, so a legitimately cached None value is indistinguishable from a miss and get_or_compute recomputes it every call.
self._local has no max-size or LRU eviction; set/warm/Redis-promotion grow it unbounded, risking memory exhaustion.
No locking: TOCTOU between the membership check (line 51) and del (line 54), plus non-atomic stats updates, can raise KeyError or corrupt counts under concurrent access.
RedisClient.setex ignores ttl_seconds so the L2 tier never expires; combined with the broken L1 check, no real TTL enforcement exists (fixture stub, likely intentional).
stats.hits conflates L1 and L2 hits with no separate accounting, limiting cache-effectiveness observability.
